summary: We purchased the Sky Tavern land on May 25, 1945, from Robison Neeman. At that time, it only had a T-bar installed in 1944 and a small building with minimal facilities. We started building the hotel in September 1945 and opened by December 15 that year, despite challenges like scarce materials and harsh weather. The area included about 800 acres, with parts later becoming Mount Rose Ski Area. It was a labor-intensive project, involving GIs from the Reno Army Air Base and lots of hand labor to install water and sewage systems.

Robison Neeman was the person we bought the Sky Tavern land from in May 1945. Before we purchased it, he had taken over the property where Wayne Poulsen and Ed Heath had installed a rope tow in 1939-40. After Neeman bought it, he operated a two-section rope tow for one season in 1941 and ran a small building serving sandwiches and hot dogs. His ownership was a key part of the site's early ski development before we expanded it.

During the construction of Sky Tavern in 1945, we faced labor shortages, so we used some GIs from the Reno Army Air Base. They worked day shifts at Sky Tavern and returned to the base for night shifts. Their help was crucial for heavy tasks like carrying water pipes and building the sewage system. This collaboration helped us meet tight deadlines despite post-war material and labor scarcity.

When we purchased the Sky Tavern property, it included land below Galena Creek at the edge of the timber. This was part of the roughly 800 acres we acquired in 1945. The area around Galena Creek was significant as it bordered the timberline and was included in our broader development plans. It was one of several parcels that made up the initial Sky Tavern holdings.

The land we bought for Sky Tavern included the parcel where Reindeer Lodge now stands. This was part of the original 800-acre purchase in 1945. At the time, it was undeveloped land, but it later became an important part of the ski area's expansion. Reindeer Lodge's location was integral to the overall property we managed.

Part of the land we bought for Sky Tavern included about 280 acres above that later became part of the Mount Rose Ski Area. This was included in the original 800-acre purchase in 1945. Our development at Sky Tavern was connected to the broader skiing region, and Mount Rose became a significant ski destination in the area. The land we owned helped shape the ski infrastructure around Mount Rose.

Wayne Poulsen was one of the early pioneers who installed a small rope tow on the hill in 1939-40, along with Ed Heath. Their rope tow ran from the old highway crossing partway up the hill. After Robison Neeman bought the property, he removed them, but their work laid the groundwork for skiing in the area. Their efforts were part of the early ski history before Sky Tavern was developed.

Ed Heath, along with Wayne Poulsen, installed a small rope tow on the hill in 1939-40. Their rope tow covered about two-thirds of the hill starting from the old highway crossing. This was one of the first ski lift efforts in the area before Robison Neeman took over the property. Their work was foundational to the skiing activity that later expanded with Sky Tavern.

Before we had ski tows locally, I skied at Donner Summit starting around 1938. They had a rope tow there that we used to get up the hill. That was one of the few places with ski lifts at the time, and it was important for early skiing experiences. It was also around then that Sugar Bowl was being built nearby, marking the start of more developed ski resorts in the Sierra Nevada.

Sugar Bowl Ski Resort began construction in 1938 and was notable for having the first chair lift in the Sierras, possibly the West. However, it was closed during the war years because access was restricted due to railroad tracks. It didn't reopen until the 1946-47 season. The community was very happy to have Sugar Bowl back, as it was a major ski destination near the area where we were developing Sky Tavern.

Reno was the city where I recruited two crews of men to help build Sky Tavern in 1945. The Chamber of Commerce in Reno was very supportive of our ski resort efforts. Many of our workers and volunteers came from Reno, including local ranchers and University of Nevada students. Reno was the hub for labor and community support during the early years of Sky Tavern.

The Reno Chamber of Commerce was very supportive of our efforts to develop Sky Tavern. They recognized the value of having a ski resort in the area and helped promote it. Their backing was important as we dealt with limited facilities and growing crowds. The Chamber played a role in encouraging tourism and local business around the ski area.

At the time we developed Sky Tavern, Incline Village was very small, with only three or four homes. The highway to Incline and Lake Tahoe was closed at Sky Tavern and couldn't be kept open, limiting access. The major development of Incline Village came much later, after the highway bypass was completed in 1960. So during our early years, Incline was a quiet, undeveloped area.

Lake Tahoe was nearby but not easily accessible from Sky Tavern in the early years because the highway was closed at Sky Tavern and couldn't be kept open to Incline and the lake. The area around Lake Tahoe was less developed then, with only a few homes at Incline Village. Over time, as roads improved and resorts grew, Lake Tahoe became a major destination, but during our start, it was still quite remote.

Some of the volunteers who helped operate Sky Tavern on weekends were students from the University of Nevada, Reno. They, along with local ranchers and their sons, contributed part-time labor to help run the ski lifts and maintain the slopes. The university community was an important source of support in those early years when staffing was limited. Their involvement helped build a local skiing culture.
